perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall commence on the date first written above and shall automatically terminate three (3) years thereafter; however, either party may terminate this Agreement at any time prior thereto upon thirty (30) days prior written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall commence on the date first written above and shall automatically terminate ten (10) years thereafter; however, either party may terminate this Agreement at any time prior thereto upon five (5) days prior written notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination notice period to five days may be unreasonably short and conflict with common law principles requiring reasonable notice, especially considering the potential disruption to the other party's business.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principle of Reasonable Notice
perturbation.law_citation: Bardal v. Globe & Mail Ltd., 1960 CanLII 294 (ON CA)
perturbation.law_explanation: The common law principle of reasonable notice dictates that a party terminating a contract must provide the other party with sufficient time to adjust to the termination. A five-day notice period is likely insufficient, particularly given the potential reliance on the Confidential Information and Intellectual Property exchanged over a period of years. The Bardal case sets out the factors considered in determining reasonable notice.

perturbation.justification: The modification of the termination notice period from thirty (30) days to five (5) days creates a potential legal contradiction based on the common law principle of reasonable notice. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this issue, stating that the five-day notice period may be unreasonably short and conflict with common law principles. The Law Explanation reinforces this by citing the Bardal case and emphasizing that sufficient time should be given to adjust to the termination. While no specific statutes are provided in the scraped snippets to define "reasonable notice", the general legal principle described in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation indicates that the reduced notice period is likely insufficient, given the context of the agreement, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party's obligations with respect to each item of the Confidential Information and Intellectual Property which it learns or receives from the other prior to the date of termination of the Agreement shall terminate three (3) years after the date of disclosure of such time of Confidential Information and Intellectual Property.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party's obligations with respect to each item of the Confidential Information and Intellectual Property which it learns or receives from the other prior to the date of termination of the Agreement shall terminate one (1) year after the date of disclosure of such time of Confidential Information and Intellectual Property.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be deemed insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially if the information has a longer commercial life. This may conflict with provincial trade secrets legislation or common law principles governing confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Ontario doesn't have specific trade secrets legislation, the common law duty of confidence protects confidential information. A one-year confidentiality period may be considered too short to adequately protect trade secrets, depending on the nature of the information and the industry. The courts will assess whether the duration is reasonable given the circumstances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Metaverse Avatars: A Pandora's Box Of Legal Issues? (Video) - Social Media - United States ARTICLE 16 April 2023 Metaverse Avatars: A Pandora's Box Of Legal Issues? (Video) G Gamma Law More Contributor Gamma Law is a specialty law firm providing premium support to select clients in cutting-edge media/tech industry sectors. We have deep expertise in video games and esports, VR/AR/XR, digital media and entertainment, cryptocurrencies and blockchain. Our clients range from founders of emerging businesses to multinational enterprises. Explore Firm Details The concept of an avatar-based metaverse is becoming increasingly popular and is adding a new dimension to the world of social media. United States Media, Telecoms, IT, Entertainment David B. Hoppe To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. The concept of an avatar-based metaverse is becoming increasingly popular and is adding a new dimension to the world of social media. Businesses can benefit by using digital avatars as part of their brand identity and for delivering a more personal user experience to their customers. However, digital avatars should be used cautiously as they come with a range of legal and regulatory issues. This article will examine regulatory concerns associated with using digital avatars in the metaverse and some potential legal solutions to minimize legal liability. Avatars: The Way of Web3 In web3, avatars are virtual representations of persons or entities in computer-generated 3D worlds, commonly used in chat and entertainment websites. AI-powered virtual assistants designed to facilitate intelligent interactions with customers are one type and application of avatar. These avatars, especially 3D ones, can be employed for various purposes, including building trust and making communication with users more direct and easier. By offering a familiar, human-like face to users, companies can communicate their values in a more personal, specific, and engaging way, potentially building brand equity and customer loyalty. When interacting with digital avatars, many users report feeling more at ease and even experiencing a release of dopamine. As such, digital avatars can be a valuable marketing tool for engaging with end users. However, it must be noted that avatars are not without their limitations. While they can evoke a genuine sense of familiarity and even empathy, they of course do not possess a human level of understanding or emotional intelligence. As such, they may not always be the most effective means of communication, particularly in more complex or emotionally charged scenarios. Despite these limitations, the use of avatars and digital assistants is becoming increasingly common in a range of applications, from the service industry to healthcare. As such, it is important for companies to carefully consider the legal and regulatory implications of their use, and to ensure that they are compliant with relevant laws and regulations. By doing so, they can take full advantage of the benefits offered by digital avatars, while minimizing associated risks. Potential Legal Solutions In addition to legal solutions mentioned in the previous section, emerging technologies companies that intend to use digital avatars can consider adopting the following legal strategies to minimize legal liability arising from introducing avatars. Terms of Service Having appropriate terms of service can help mitigate legal liability. Perhaps, just like Massively multiplayer online role-playing games (MMORPGs), emerging technologies companies can have terms of service that govern the conduct of avatars contractually. It can also include remedies for violations such as banning the avatar from the platform and confiscating virtual assets. Confiscation of virtual assets could potentially be a solid remedy for violations as these in-world assets may be traded with other users (or avatars) and can ultimately be cashed out. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this may be insufficient to protect trade secrets and could conflict with common law principles. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that a one-year confidentiality period might be too short to adequately protect trade secrets, depending on the information and industry. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Snippet 2 discusses digital avatars and legal solutions. While not directly on point, it emphasizes the importance of terms of service to mitigate legal liability in emerging technologies. The change creates a potential legal contradiction because the shortened period may not provide reasonable protection for confidential information, which the courts would assess based on the specific circumstances. This directly undermines the protection intended under common law duty of confidence, as a one-year term may be deemed insufficient for many trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: XII. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of New Brunswick.
perturbation.changed_text: XII. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware, USA, might be problematic if the parties and the subject matter of the contract are primarily connected to Canada (New Brunswick). This could lead to a conflict of laws issue and potentially render certain provisions unenforceable under Canadian law.
perturbation.law_citation: Choice of Law Rules in Contract (Canada)
perturbation.law_explanation: Canadian conflict of laws principles generally respect the parties' choice of law, but this is not absolute. A Canadian court might disregard the choice of Delaware law if the contract has no substantial connection to Delaware and applying Delaware law would violate Canadian public policy or render the agreement unfair. The court would consider the location of the parties, the subject matter of the contract, and the potential impact on the weaker party.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies the laws of the Province of New Brunswick. The changed text replaces this with the laws of the State of Delaware. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that this change might be problematic if the contract has stronger connections to Canada than Delaware. The Law Explanation states that a Canadian court might disregard the choice of Delaware law if the contract has no substantial connection to Delaware and applying Delaware law would violate Canadian public policy or render the agreement unfair. This creates an inconsistency as it introduces a potentially unenforceable clause. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 relates to the interpretation of statutes and regulations in Canada, its applicability is related to general interpretation. As such it supports that all laws must be interpreted in light of the relevant statues and regulations. Because the contract specifies a choice of law that may be unenforceable, it creates a legal inconsistency, which is defined as a type of legal contradiction.
